jokes.text: Abbi waking up confused: 'What? How am I getting shorter?'
jokes.setup: Opening cold before any context is established — the audience has no idea what's happening
jokes.punchline: The disoriented non-sequitur 'How am I getting shorter?' reveals she's mid-dream without telling us she's dreaming
jokes.explanation: Effective cold open disorientation. 'How am I getting shorter?' is a funny specific detail for a dream state — not just 'where am I' but a physics-defying observation. Gets funnier in retrospect once the sexy dream is revealed. Not exceptional but a solid hook.

jokes.text: Jeremy tells Abbi she'd be the person he'd give a spare key to, and Abbi immediately crumbles into flustered stammering about 'commitment'
jokes.setup: Jeremy makes an offhand comment that functions as a massive romantic compliment — she'd be the one he trusts with his key
jokes.punchline: Abbi's response is the opposite of smooth — 'I would take one. If we want to go through that and that commitment, I would have it with me whenever.' Pure awkward overcorrection.
jokes.explanation: Strong character comedy. Abbi's inability to just accept a compliment without spiraling into 'commitment' language is instantly specific and recognizable. The phrase 'if we want to go through that and that commitment' is beautifully awkward — too formal, too much, all wrong. This is Abbi's defining social flaw expressed in a single fumbled sentence.

jokes.text: Jeremy asks 'Do you mind if I take off my shirt?' and Abbi says 'Where'd you get all that oil from?'
jokes.setup: Dream logic: Jeremy takes off his shirt to put up a shelf — already absurd — but Abbi's brain has him oiled up
jokes.punchline: 'Where'd you get all that oil from?' — the dream is so transparently horny that even within it Abbi's subconscious is self-aware enough to notice the impossibility
jokes.explanation: Funny because it respects dream logic while skewering it. The question treats the oiled body as a real-world mystery to be solved — a perfect absurdist beat. Economy is excellent: four words do all the work. The self-aware confusion within the dream is the kind of specific comic detail that elevates this above generic 'sexy dream' humor.

jokes.text: 'Now I can put up that shelf.' — Jeremy, dripping with oil, announces the shelf's purpose as though it's totally normal
jokes.setup: The whole sequence has been building absurdly oily dream logic
jokes.punchline: The mundane callback to the original task ('put up a shelf') delivered as if the oil is just a functional prerequisite
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. Six words. The deadpan logic of using oil as a prerequisite for carpentry is structurally tight — it honors the setup (he did come to help with a shelf) while being completely insane. The joke understands its own absurdist rules.

jokes.text: 'You're so... slippery.' — Abbi, apparently touching oiled Jeremy
jokes.setup: Dream is escalating; audience expects something more explicitly sexual
jokes.punchline: What she settles on, after the ellipsis, is 'slippery' — the least sexy adjective available, which makes it funnier than anything overtly dirty
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection. The pause ('You're so...') sets up an obvious beat for something overtly sexual; 'slippery' is technically accurate but comedically deflating in the best possible way. It's also perfect character work — even in her own fantasy, Abbi doesn't fully commit to the moment. High economy, genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Abbi wakes up and Bevers says 'Someone had a sexy dream, huh?' — implying he witnessed or heard something
jokes.setup: Abbi wakes from a clearly very involved dream
jokes.punchline: Bevers, her annoying roommate's boyfriend who shouldn't even be there, is immediately aware and commenting on it
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely about Bevers being the worst possible person to have witness this. It's structurally sound and character-specific — Bevers's intrusive presence is his entire joke. The cringe is earned.

jokes.text: Abbi wakes from the dream and we hear an adjacent male voice narrating: 'My neighbor Abbi is so sexy and now I have this great big humongous boner'
jokes.setup: Abbi wakes from a hot dream — we assume the post-dream commentary will be hers, or at least from someone she finds attractive
jokes.punchline: It's Bevers, her slovenly roommate's freeloading boyfriend, who has apparently also been having a parallel sex dream about Abbi — and his narration is hilariously artless ('great big humongous boner')
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best structural joke in the cold open. The misdirection is clean: we think this is Abbi's moment of sexual satisfaction, and then Bevers crashes it with maximum gracelessness. 'Great big humongous boner' is redundant in the best possible way — 'great big' AND 'humongous' is a child's vocabulary applied to something gross. The structure is nearly perfect: sexy dream → Abbi wakes → Bevers ruins it. Strong 7.9.

jokes.text: Ilana immediately proposes: 'I was thinking maybe we could masturbate next to each other.' Then adds it's 'parallel play.'
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives seemingly to support Abbi after her dream — a normal friend check-in
jokes.punchline: Her opening offer is mutual adjacent masturbation, framed in the innocent developmental-psychology term 'parallel play'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Parallel play' is the key: it's a term for toddlers playing side-by-side without direct interaction, which makes applying it to mutual masturbation both hilarious and weirdly apt as a description of Ilana's philosophy of friendship. The concept is original, the vocabulary choice is inspired, and it is completely and only a thing Ilana would say. High marks across the board. Justifies the 8+ score because the joke has genuine conceptual novelty and executes it with precision.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'It's because I want to so badly.' — confessing she's always wanted to parallel-masturbate with Ilana
jokes.setup: Ilana has always been proposing this and Abbi has always rejected it; we assume Abbi finds it uncomfortable
jokes.punchline: The real reason Abbi never does it is that she wants to too badly — implying the desire itself is the problem, possibly because she's afraid of what it means
jokes.explanation: The inversion is sharp: we expect 'I'm too uncomfortable' and get 'I want it too much.' This is the show's central friendship-as-romance dynamic compressed into one line. It deepens both characters in about eight words. Excellent economy and character integration.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'What a way to start the day.' Ilana: 'It's 5 p.m.' Abbi: 'Great... Closing time.'
jokes.setup: Abbi celebrates waking up as if it's morning
jokes.punchline: It's 5 PM. Rather than embarrassment, Abbi reframes the entire day as 'closing time' — a complete philosophical pivot
jokes.explanation: 'Closing time' is the payoff — it's not just an excuse, it's a complete restructuring of reality to avoid shame. Very consistent with Abbi's character and delivered with the show's characteristic deadpan. Efficient three-line exchange.

jokes.text: Hurricane prep supplies include 'Sour Patch Kids, Nutter Butters, Mrs. Fields cookies' — and 'potato salad half off because we all might die'
jokes.setup: They were sent to get emergency hurricane supplies — batteries, duct tape, the basics
jokes.punchline: The disaster prep kit is almost entirely junk food, with the justification that mortality makes discounted potato salad appropriate
jokes.explanation: Solid Ilana joke. The logic — 'we might die so normal shopping rules don't apply' — is a real and relatable impulse exaggerated to its absurd conclusion. The specific brand names ground it. 'Half off' is a funny detail because it implies financial frugality survives even existential crisis.

jokes.text: Bevers's sister has an 11th toe, and 'it's not where you would expect it to be'
jokes.setup: Abbi is already upset that Bevers invited his sister; this is the horror detail that tops it
jokes.punchline: Not just a bonus toe — a bonus toe in an unexpected location. The mystery of where is left to the audience's imagination, which is the correct choice.
jokes.explanation: The joke lives in what isn't said. 'Not where you would expect it to be' activates the audience's imagination in the worst possible way — the non-location is funnier than any specific answer could be. Good structural instinct to leave it ambiguous. Will pay off later with 'tell it to the toe.'

jokes.text: Marla enters saying 'Do I smell potato salad? Just kidding, I know I smell potato salad.'
jokes.setup: First appearance of Marla Bevers — we're primed for her to be eccentric
jokes.punchline: Her intro joke is a self-aware retraction of a rhetorical question — she never actually had doubt, she was just performing social niceties about her potato salad knowledge
jokes.explanation: A good character intro that establishes Marla as someone who operates just slightly off normal social rails. The self-correction ('just kidding, I know I smell it') is a specific and funny quirk. It's not a huge joke but it's economical and it establishes character efficiently.

jokes.text: Marla claims the potato salad was 'a gift for the host of the party' — commandeering supplies she didn't bring
jokes.setup: Abbi's supplies are being socially requisitioned by a guest who just arrived
jokes.punchline: Marla reframes Abbi's emergency food as a gift she, the guest, is graciously receiving on behalf of hospitality norms
jokes.explanation: Functional character joke continuing Marla's oblivious self-centeredness. Not particularly original but consistent and well-deployed for character establishment.

jokes.text: Bevers refuses to go to his girlfriend's parents' house because 'it's too much presh. Her parents are like, too into me. They just think I'm really fun.'
jokes.setup: His girlfriend evacuated for a hurricane and invited him; he stayed behind at someone else's apartment
jokes.punchline: The excuse is that her parents like him too much — he's avoiding affection, not conflict. Delivered with complete sincerity and zero self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Bevers character joke. His reasoning is an inversion of every normal excuse — usually people flee in-laws who don't like them. Bevers is so comfortably oblivious that he can't handle being appreciated. 'Too much presh' is a perfectly calibrated Bevers-ism. 'The fun's staying here!' is the capper — he's literally choosing a hurricane over love. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'It's like camp right now!' — Bevers treating a hurricane and an unwanted houseguest situation as summer fun
jokes.setup: Abbi is clearly miserable about this invasion of her apartment
jokes.punchline: Bevers is living his best life and frames it in the most innocent, childhood-joy register possible
jokes.explanation: Rewatch bonus: 'camp' as a concept pays off significantly later with Bevers's rap, the counselor Danny reveal, and 'light as a feather.' This line plants the seed. On its own, it's a functional character joke. Its value is largely structural.

jokes.text: Abbi's hurricane confession: she switched their laptop chargers in the night 'like a sneaky thief' and that's why Ilana's charger never works
jokes.setup: 'In case we all die, there is something I must confess' — big dramatic buildup suggesting a significant secret
jokes.punchline: The confession is that she switched laptop chargers. The 'crime' is minuscule. The moral weight she's placed on it is enormous.
jokes.explanation: Excellent bathos. 'In case we all die' primes the audience for something devastating; 'I switched our chargers' is a perfect deflation. But then the joke keeps going: 'I see you try and you try and you try and it's because it's mine. It's never gonna work, Ilana.' The extended detail of watching Ilana fail while knowing the secret is what elevates this from a simple anticlimax to genuine guilt comedy. Strong character integration — this is completely Abbi.

jokes.text: Ilana forgives Abbi instantly: 'It's not a big deal at all.' Abbi: 'You are forgiveness. You are. Wow. Whew. Thank you everybody. Namaste. Gracias.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has been building to this confession with enormous guilt
jokes.punchline: Ilana doesn't care at all. Abbi's relief is so extreme she thanks the entire room in multiple languages for no reason.
jokes.explanation: 'Namaste. Gracias.' is the capper — the relief has become so excessive it has turned into a mini-ceremony. 'Thank you everybody' when nobody did anything is a perfect absurdist grace note. Strong Abbi character work.

jokes.text: Never Have I Ever: 'Never have I ever read a newspaper.' — Ilana. Response: 'Even homeless people read newspapers.'
jokes.setup: Never Have I Ever drinking game — expected to produce juicy revelations
jokes.punchline: Ilana's contribution is so baseline-low that someone points out it undercuts even the unhoused
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy. Never Have I Ever is supposed to surface hidden behavior, but Ilana's is so mundane and socially low-status that the joke is the complete failure of the format. The comeback ('even homeless people read newspapers') is the right punchline — a gentle reality check that lands as both burn and accurate social observation.

jokes.text: Never Have I Ever follow-up: 'Never have I ever dealt with how my parents' divorce affected my overall, you know, being in relationships.'
jokes.setup: A drinking game should produce fun confessions; the previous entry was too mundane
jokes.punchline: This one goes too deep — it's not a secret, it's an unprocessed trauma delivered as a party game contribution
jokes.explanation: Decent cringe joke about the volatility of party games with near-strangers. The pivot from 'too mundane' to 'too personal' is structurally smart — the game is failing in both directions simultaneously. Less specific than the other jokes in this sequence though.

jokes.text: 'Light as a feather, stiff as a board' — Ilana initiates a full camp levitation ritual during the hurricane party
jokes.setup: Bevers said it's 'like camp,' so Ilana is literalizing the camp vibe
jokes.punchline: She fully commits to the childhood ritual in the middle of a hurricane party, getting everyone to use two fingers to lift her
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'camp' pays off here and throughout the episode. The levitation ritual is a genuinely funny choice because it's both age-inappropriate and perfectly sincere — Ilana would absolutely do this. Physical comedy that works in the transcript because the dialogue scaffolds it clearly.

jokes.text: 'Just a lot of people here and I spite eat.' — Abbi explaining why she consumed the entire potato salad
jokes.setup: Someone notices something is wrong with Abbi during the levitation game
jokes.punchline: 'Spite eat' — a perfectly coined term for a very specific and relatable behavior, delivered with total matter-of-fact calm
jokes.explanation: 'Spite eat' is a coined term that feels immediately true — everyone knows this behavior but no one had named it quite this way. The economy is exceptional: two words does what a full sentence of explanation would fail to do. Highly quotable. This is the kind of small verbal invention that shows at their best produce regularly. Justifies the high score because it's genuinely original vocabulary that names a universal experience.

jokes.text: Abbi refuses to use the bathroom because there are too many people. Ilana offers to come in and do 'ocean noises in your ear to relax you.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has a poop anxiety around crowds — a universal if embarrassing problem
jokes.punchline: Ilana's first solution is to accompany her and perform ambient sound design in her ear to help her relax
jokes.explanation: Pure Ilana. The offer to provide live ocean noise as an auditory aid to bathroom relaxation is completely genuine and completely insane. It also frames a bathroom activity in the gentle, therapeutic language of wellness retreats. Maximum character integration — no other character on any other show says this.

jokes.text: 'Number two for number two.' — Ilana's response when Abbi chooses Option Two (distraction)
jokes.setup: Ilana presents two options to help Abbi with her bathroom anxiety
jokes.punchline: When Abbi picks option two, Ilana notes that it's option two for a number two — a pun that works on two levels and is announced with zero fanfare
jokes.explanation: Elegant wordplay. The double meaning is constructed by circumstance (two options, one being number two) and Ilana notices and names it with perfect comic timing. The deadpan 'nice' that she presumably delivers makes it land clean. Highly quotable. The joke is structurally tight and earns its cleverness rather than reaching for it.

jokes.text: Ilana's distraction: 'Bags! Throw 'em up in the air, huh? It's like American Beauty.'
jokes.setup: Ilana needs to distract everyone so Abbi can use the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Her distraction technique is throwing bags in the air and making a pretentious film reference to justify it
jokes.explanation: The American Beauty reference is funny because it's a completely sincere highbrow gloss on what is basically just 'throw stuff in the air.' Ilana would absolutely invoke a prestige film to justify an absurd distraction. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'On the one night that Jeremy comes over to my apartment, I explode.' — Abbi using 'explode' to describe her digestive situation to Ilana
jokes.setup: Abbi is distressed about the timing of her bathroom crisis relative to Jeremy's presence
jokes.punchline: 'I explode' — the word choice is simultaneously a normal expression of emotional overwhelm and a very literal description of what happened in the bathroom
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay that works within context but isn't remarkable. The double meaning is apparent but not especially crafted — it's more of an incidental pun than a designed one. Functional.

jokes.text: 'This is the best. This is a sexy situation. It's terrifying outside. There's candles all lit. And who's your competition? Marla Bevers?'
jokes.setup: Abbi thinks the bathroom disaster has ruined her night with Jeremy
jokes.punchline: Ilana reframes it as a romantic opportunity, noting that Abbi's only competition for Jeremy's attention is Marla Bevers — who is by definition not competition
jokes.explanation: Good Ilana pep talk comedy. The logic is both encouraging and devastating — yes, you have no competition, but that's because your competition is a woman with a misplaced 11th toe. 'And who's your competition? Marla Bevers?' lands as both a compliment and a diss simultaneously. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'I am a doo doo ninja.'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks what Ilana's plan is for dealing with the toilet situation
jokes.punchline: Ilana declares herself a 'doo doo ninja' — a self-awarded title for covert fecal disposal
jokes.explanation: Four words that are maximally funny and maximally Ilana. 'Doo doo ninja' combines the infantile (doo doo) with the badass (ninja) in a way that is simultaneously disgusting and heroic — which is exactly Ilana's self-image. This is the most quotable line in the episode and one of the show's quintessential Ilana moments. The setup ('What does that even mean?') from Abbi is the perfect reaction. High impact because of delivery context and pure quotability.

jokes.text: Ilana demands eye contact for permission: 'Look me in the eyes and tell me I have your permission.' / 'Any means necessary?'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks permission to handle the situation 'by any means necessary'
jokes.punchline: The escalating formality of the permission ritual — eye contact, verbal confirmation, repetition of terms — makes it feel like a heist or a surgery consent form, for a poop disposal
jokes.explanation: Funny because of the tonal mismatch — the gravity and formality of the consent ritual versus the subject matter. 'Any means necessary' takes on a completely different register when said about covert fecal disposal. The payoff (shoe) makes this retroactively much funnier.

jokes.text: 'Now get out there, bounce some little titties for that hunky, quarter-Latino and whatever else he is.'
jokes.setup: Ilana sends Abbi out to make her move on Jeremy
jokes.punchline: The pep talk is 'bounce some little titties' plus a characterization of Jeremy by ethnicity that ends in 'whatever else he is' — both raunchy and casually clueless in a very Ilana way
jokes.explanation: 'Quarter-Latino and whatever else he is' is the funny part — Ilana has partially researched Jeremy's background and then ran out of interest. The specific fraction ('quarter') suggests she knows something specific, which makes 'whatever else he is' funnier as an abandonment of effort. The 'bounce some little titties' is crude but consistent with Ilana as cheerleader. Repeated later as a callback.

jokes.text: Abbi explains she was in her bedroom because 'a felf shell.' She then corrects: 'A shelf fell.'
jokes.setup: Abbi needs to explain her absence to Jeremy without revealing the bathroom situation
jokes.punchline: 'A felf shell' — she's so nervous that she transposes the consonants in 'shelf fell,' then has to walk it back
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe moment. 'A felf shell' is a perfect verbal stumble — specific enough to be funny, close enough to the correct words to be recognizable, and instantly revealing of how nervous she is. The callback to the shelf from her dream (and Jeremy's earlier offer to help with a shelf) adds a layer. Jeremy's response 'I'm pretty good with the felf shells' is also charming and would generate warmth in the room.

jokes.text: Jeremy says 'I'm pretty good with the felf shells' — echoing Abbi's malapropism back at her gently
jokes.setup: Abbi has just embarrassed herself with 'felf shell'
jokes.punchline: Jeremy plays along using her wrong words, which simultaneously signals he noticed, he doesn't mind, and he's charming about it
jokes.explanation: Jeremy's echo-callback turns a cringe moment into a sweet one, which is its own comedic register — the relief laugh. It also develops Jeremy as a character who responds to Abbi's awkwardness with warmth rather than embarrassment, which makes the audience root for him.

jokes.text: Marla cuts off the romantic moment: 'Don't have to figure out what your talent is, Ab. Pretty sure it's not washing dishes. She's always making me wash dishes!'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Jeremy are having a moment; Marla interrupts to announce a talent show and immediately roast Abbi
jokes.punchline: The interruption is bad enough; the roast lands in the worst possible place — accusing Abbi of being a bad roommate in front of her crush
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe comedy. Marla as chaos agent is well-used here — her timing couldn't be worse. 'She's always making me wash dishes!' as the thing she chooses to share is a specific and believable overshare. Not remarkable but does its job.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Such a jokester. I love dishes and washing them.' — forced recovery through aggressive normalizing
jokes.setup: Marla has just told Jeremy that Abbi makes her wash dishes
jokes.punchline: Abbi's cover story — 'I love dishes and washing them' — is delivered with such unconvincing enthusiasm that it makes everything worse
jokes.explanation: Abbi's panicked cover attempt is perfectly calibrated to fail — nobody has ever said 'I love dishes and washing them' with sincerity, and its very formulation reveals the lie. Good cringe texture.

jokes.text: Ilana returns from the bathroom task: 'It is gone. The deed is done.' — delivered as if completing a military operation
jokes.setup: Ilana was sent to dispose of Abbi's bathroom situation 'by any means necessary'
jokes.punchline: Her return report uses the formal language of covert operations for what was apparently a successful poop disposal
jokes.explanation: 'The deed is done' is a perfect tonal mismatch — it's the language of assassination or prophecy applied to sanitation. The interrupted word before the singing ('Your sh—') teases the reveal of what 'the deed' actually involved. Ilana's complete seriousness is the joke.

jokes.text: Ilana improvises a song about the talent show: '♪ It looks like there's a talent show / In the living room / I think that we should go do that now ♪'
jokes.setup: Ilana needs to redirect Abbi and Jeremy toward the talent show
jokes.punchline: Rather than just saying 'talent show,' Ilana sings a completely literal, unlyrical song describing exactly what is happening in real time
jokes.explanation: The joke is that the song contains zero artistic invention — it's just the facts of the situation set to melody. 'I think that we should go do that now' is a hilariously banal lyric. Strong Ilana character work. The follow-up comment 'I'll do anything if she sings it like that' from Jeremy suggests it works on him, which is also funny.

jokes.text: Jeremy: 'I'll do anything if she sings it like that.' — responding to Ilana's absurd song
jokes.setup: Ilana has just sung a monotone, literal description of events
jokes.punchline: Jeremy is completely won over by it — which makes Ilana's charisma inexplicably effective and Jeremy charming
jokes.explanation: A good reaction beat that does double duty: it's funny that Ilana's nonsense song works, and it develops Jeremy as someone with good humor. Efficient.

jokes.text: Talent show: 'Hello, my name is Flo. Everyone say, Hello, Flo. I can do lots of dances. I can do the running man. I can do the Roger Rabbit.'
jokes.setup: A talent show has been assembled among this random group of hurricane party guests
jokes.punchline: The first performer treats the crowd like kindergarteners, introduces herself with a call-and-response, and announces 90s dances as talents
jokes.explanation: Funny mainly because of the gap between 'talent show' as a concept and the actual 'talent' being displayed. The Roger Rabbit and running man are period-specific choices that signal this person learned their moves and hasn't updated since. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Bevers's camp rap includes the line '♪ We like to eat Skittles and master the bait... ting of hooks and archery too ♪'
jokes.setup: Bevers is performing his camp rap as his talent; it sounds like a normal camp song
jokes.punchline: 'Master the bait' breaks across a line in a way that makes 'masturbate' audible before the rap pivots to '...ting of hooks and archery' — a deliberate near-miss that's funnier than saying it outright
jokes.explanation: Expertly structured dirty wordplay. The line break is perfectly timed — 'master the bait' hangs in the air for exactly long enough before the innocent completion. It's funnier for not going through with it, and the subsequent line '♪ Not just with our mouths but with our mushroom caps ♪' (which is much more explicit) creates an escalation that retroactively makes the earlier near-miss even funnier. Good structural craft.

jokes.text: 'Not just with our mouths but with our mushroom caps ♪' — the camp rap escalates to explicit territory
jokes.setup: The rap has been doing a near-miss with 'masterbait'; people think they know where this is going
jokes.punchline: 'Mushroom caps' is explicitly anatomical — the rap has gone from suggestive to unmistakable. 'No, no! The schlong song part is the best part of the rap!'
jokes.explanation: The escalation from near-miss to explicit is well-constructed, and the capper 'the schlong song part is the best part of the rap!' reframes everything — there's an entire section called the schlong song, and he's proud of it. 'Schlong song' itself is funny as a coinage. This is Bevers at his best: genuinely enthusiastic about something deeply inappropriate.

jokes.text: 'I got a boot in my cootch and I do not care!'
jokes.setup: The leg wrestling match is apparently physical enough that someone's foot has ended up in an anatomically improbable location
jokes.punchline: 'I do not care!' — Ilana's defining characteristic is complete comfort with her own body, and she announces this invasion cheerfully and at full volume
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable and maximally Ilana. The rhyme of 'boot' and 'cootch' makes it feel like a battle cry. The 'I do not care!' is the punchline — it's Ilana's entire character distilled into a response to physical discomfort. This is the kind of line that gets clipped and shared. Economy is excellent: eleven words that are entirely specific to this character.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'My talent can't be to like, wash his body.' Ilana: 'You mean like, wash his body after sex, right? I want to make sure you're optimizing your fantasies.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to figure out how to use the talent show to get closer to Jeremy
jokes.punchline: Ilana misunderstands (or understands perfectly) and asks for clarification to make sure Abbi's sexual imagination is operating at peak efficiency
jokes.explanation: 'Optimizing your fantasies' is a great Ilana coinage — she treats Abbi's sexual imagination as a resource to be managed and maximized, which is completely her. The clarification question (post-sex vs. pre-sex washing) is also funny for its specificity.

jokes.text: Truth or Dare scheme: Ilana will say 'dare,' get dared to do something embarrassing, which will give Abbi cover to do something with Jeremy
jokes.setup: Ilana hatches a plan to use Truth or Dare to engineer a romantic moment
jokes.punchline: The plan is immediately cut off by 'got it, got it, got it, say no more' — Abbi understands without needing the rest, which means the audience doesn't hear the specifics, which is funnier
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in what's not said — their shared understanding of the plan implies it's something that doesn't need to be spoken aloud, which primes the audience. The setup for the eventual dare reveal.

jokes.text: Ilana announces she won leg wrestling: 'Don't feel bad. I'm on speed.'
jokes.setup: The leg wrestling match concludes with Ilana winning
jokes.punchline: Her sportsmanship involves immediately revealing she was on speed, which recontextualizes the entire competition
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. Five words of consolation followed by three words of revelation that invalidate the entire competition. 'Don't feel bad. I'm on speed.' is structurally tight and maximally Ilana — she says it as casually as 'I had a good breakfast.' The brevity is the joke. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Truth or Dare: Abbi picks 'dare.' Ilana: 'I dare you to suck Jeremy's [bleep].'
jokes.setup: The Truth or Dare scheme was supposed to be subtle — Ilana would take the dare, something would happen organically
jokes.punchline: Ilana completely abandons the plan and just dares Abbi directly to perform a sex act on Jeremy, in front of everyone, including Jeremy
jokes.explanation: The callback-to-betrayal is the joke: the plan was for Ilana to take the dare. Instead she pivots to blunt directness and just says the thing. Abbi's response 'Um, that's a wrap on Truth or Dare' is the perfect straight-man recovery. The scene succeeds because it subverts the scheme it just established. Jeremy is standing right there.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Um, that's a wrap on Truth or Dare.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just publicly dared Abbi to perform a sex act on her crush
jokes.punchline: Abbi ends the game with the calm production-speak of someone wrapping a film shoot
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy. 'That's a wrap on Truth or Dare' is six words that convey mortification, authority, and exhaustion simultaneously. The production-speak register ('that's a wrap') implies she's directing her own humiliation out of existence. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Reaction to Bevers's bedroom setup — 'That pasty ass. He lives here for free.' / 'Fully stocked mini fridge.'
jokes.setup: Characters wander into Bevers's corner of the apartment
jokes.punchline: Bevers has built out a comfortable squatter existence — a mini fridge, presumably a bed, maximum comfort — in someone else's apartment for free
jokes.explanation: Solid visual joke that summarizes Bevers's character without dialogue. The fully stocked mini fridge is the detail that does it — he's not just crashing, he's nested. 'He lives here for free' is the explicit statement of what the audience already suspects.

jokes.text: 'Should I put my Lincoln Log on one of these cans? That is so sweet. Abbi would love it.'
jokes.setup: Bevers is in his mini fridge area; someone suggests a gesture toward Abbi
jokes.punchline: Bevers considers placing a Lincoln Log on a can as a thoughtful gift, and affirms that Abbi would love this. His idea of generosity is a wooden toy balanced on a beverage container.
jokes.explanation: Odd bit that works because of its specificity — Lincoln Log is an unusual object to have, and placing it on a can is a very specific form of non-gift. The logic of Bevers genuinely thinking Abbi would love this is the joke. Moderately funny but not structurally tight.

jokes.text: 'Should I put my Lincoln Log on one of these cans? That is so sweet. Abbi would love it.'
jokes.setup: They're looking at Bevers' fully stocked mini-fridge
jokes.punchline: The neighbor wants to place a Lincoln Log (a child's toy) on a can of food as a gift for Abbi — and Ilana genuinely thinks this is sweet
jokes.explanation: The fact that Lincoln Log is a children's toy and placing one on a can of beans is a specific kind of weird gift is mildly funny. Not a strong joke.

jokes.text: Pre-death confession: 'I once ate a corn on the cob... including the cob.'
jokes.setup: Hurricane death confessions continue — we're primed for something real
jokes.punchline: The confession is eating a corn cob. Not the corn, the cob. The bathos is total.
jokes.explanation: Good bathos joke. 'Including the cob' is the punchline — the pause after 'corn on the cob' sets up a redirect that never comes. The follow-up reactions ('I don't see anything wrong with that,' 'it's roughage') are good because they refuse to treat it as a confession at all, which is also funny. Then the capper: 'I do it every time.' It's not a one-off — it's a lifestyle.

jokes.text: 'I do it every time.' — the cob eater clarifies this is habitual, not a one-time incident
jokes.setup: The corn cob confession was presented as a shameful one-time event
jokes.punchline: 'Every time' — it's not a regrettable anomaly, it's regular behavior, which makes the confession meaningless and funnier
jokes.explanation: Three words as a topper to the cob confession. 'I do it every time' means there's no confession here — this is just how this person eats corn. The escalation is minimal but well-timed.

jokes.text: Someone asks if anyone smells something; the smell is identified as 'dookie' — but it's blamed on Jaime, who stepped in something
jokes.setup: Ilana has theoretically disposed of the bathroom situation; the audience doesn't know how
jokes.punchline: A smell emerges, and the blame is quickly assigned to Jaime's shoe — but the audience will later learn the smell IS in a shoe, just not Jaime's
jokes.explanation: Strong dramatic irony joke — the audience is ahead of the characters in a way that makes the misdirection funny. 'You smell terrible' being said to someone who is innocent (Jaime) while the actual culprit (Ilana, via the shoe) is present is structurally elegant. The rewatch bonus is high because on second viewing, the whole scene is a perfectly constructed deception.

jokes.text: Ilana's attempt to cover: 'I think that we can all agree that there's no smell. It's the storm, I think.'
jokes.setup: People are smelling poop; Ilana knows exactly why
jokes.punchline: Ilana's misdirection — 'we can all agree there's no smell, it's the storm' — is transparently absurd and would convince no one
jokes.explanation: Ilana covering for her failed ninja mission is good but the line itself ('it's the storm') is a bit slack. The comedy is mostly in the situation rather than the specific writing.

jokes.text: 'Sweet dreams on my ass, Bevers!' followed by physical comedy suggesting Abbi dumps something on him
jokes.setup: Bevers has been an ongoing irritant all episode; Abbi reaches a breaking point
jokes.punchline: Whatever Abbi does physically — presumably dumping liquid or food on Bevers — is telegraphed by the triumphant 'Sweet dreams on my ass!'
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that's hard to fully assess without video, but the transcript context (exclamation, then 'Yes! Got it!') suggests something satisfying happened. 'Sweet dreams on my ass' is a Malapropism/misquote of 'sweet dreams' as a taunt. Functional but limited by transcript.

jokes.text: 'It's tea time, bitch. Oh, governor! I have a hole in my weenus!'
jokes.setup: There's some kind of British tea roleplay or costume happening
jokes.punchline: 'Weenus' — the anatomical term for elbow skin — is deployed alongside 'bitch' and 'governor' in a way that's either a malapropism for something ruder or a deliberately comic mix of registers
jokes.explanation: Funny mainly because 'weenus' sounds like a rude word but is actually the correct anatomical term for elbow skin. 'Oh governor!' is a Cockney exclamation that clashes with 'bitch.' The tonal mix is the joke. Limited by the transcript context — would land harder visually.

jokes.text: Jeremy and Abbi bond over cooking; Jeremy admits his specialty is 'Egg, mostly.' (singular)
jokes.setup: Jeremy has been described as smelling his neighbors' cooking; he asks what Abbi makes. There's been mutual escalation of food enthusiasm ('spices all over stuff and mixed in').
jokes.punchline: Jeremy's culinary specialty is 'Egg, mostly.' Not eggs — egg. The singular and the 'mostly' do all the work.
jokes.explanation: Two words. 'Egg, mostly.' The singular 'egg' vs. 'eggs' implies either he's very precise about cooking one egg or he's not even confident in that. 'Mostly' undermines the already minimal claim — sometimes not even egg. The structure (mutual building of cooking enthusiasm → complete deflation) is tight. This is a quiet gem: maximally economical, earned by the setup, and very quotable. Justifies the 7.8 because it's a masterwork of understatement in two words.

jokes.text: 'Who [bleep] in my shoe?' — Marla discovers the contents of her Shapely
jokes.setup: The audience has known about the shoe since Ilana declared 'the deed is done'; the smell scene built toward this
jokes.punchline: The payoff of the entire doo-doo ninja subplot — delivered as a furious discovery
jokes.explanation: The payoff of a very well-constructed joke infrastructure. The impact is high because the audience has been waiting for this since 'any means necessary' — the tension of knowing what's in the shoe while watching the smell scene is what makes the reveal land so hard. The follow-up 'Mother[bleep]! So which one of you maggots did it?' is perfectly over-the-top Marla. 'Shapely' as the shoe brand name is also a funny detail — it implies the shoe is named to make its wearer feel good about themselves.

jokes.text: 'No one's going anywhere until I find out who took a (bleep) in my Shapely!'
jokes.setup: Marla has discovered her shoe situation
jokes.punchline: Marla's response is to lock down the apartment like a crime scene — 'Shapely' as the branded shoe name adds specificity that makes the outrage funnier
jokes.explanation: The word 'Shapely' is the comedic detail — it's a specific brand name for what appears to be an orthopedic or wide-width shoe, and that specificity makes Marla's rage more sympathetic and thus funnier. Her detective-fiction response ('no one's going anywhere') is proportionate to no real situation.

jokes.text: 'Was it you, Dimples? No, I'm on a low-fiber diet. I go like, twice a week, tops. I promise.'
jokes.setup: Marla is interrogating each guest about the shoe contents
jokes.punchline: The accused's alibi is their fiber intake and weekly bathroom frequency — a completely TMI and specific denial
jokes.explanation: The alibi joke is funny because of its specificity — 'twice a week, tops' is exactly the kind of over-precise detail that makes an alibi feel both convincing and too much. 'I promise' as the closer is the right beat: it adds earnestness that makes the oversharing worse.

jokes.text: Bevers's camp defense: 'Camp was rough. Tonight brought up a lot of bad memories.' Implication: he pooped in a shoe at camp.
jokes.setup: Marla accuses Bevers; he denies and claims camp trauma
jokes.punchline: Bevers implies that shoe-pooping at camp was a real event, just not tonight — 'it wasn't me at camp either' followed by 'camp was the best'
jokes.explanation: The contradiction in Bevers's denial — 'it wasn't me at camp either' (implies it happened at camp) followed by 'camp was the best' — is perfectly constructed. He's both denying the camp incident and defending camp as an institution. His camp nostalgia is completely sincere, which makes the implied shoe-pooping at camp funnier.

jokes.text: Bevers: 'Only me and Greg Shapiro could blow ourselves. I was always the fastest at it. Always!'
jokes.setup: Bevers is defending camp as the best time of his life
jokes.punchline: The sentence 'only me and Greg Shapiro could blow ourselves' sounds sexually explicit but he's talking about... something else at camp (possibly blowing horns, or a game). His pride in being the fastest adds to the apparent wrongness.
jokes.explanation: Excellent sustained ambiguity. The sentence is maximally suggestive but Bevers's clarification makes it worse, not better: 'I would blow myself and Greg would blow himself separate / across the room. There's a video of it!' The 'across the room' is the key detail that should clarify it's something physical/athletic but instead just makes the image stranger. 'There's a video of it!' adds urgency. This is the camp callback paying off at full power — all the camp setup from 'it's like camp right now' has been building to the revelation that whatever happened at Camp Miniwakaga was deeply weird.

jokes.text: Bevers clarifies the self-blowing: 'I would blow myself and Greg would blow himself separate, across the room. There's a video of it! There's a...'
jokes.setup: The audience (and other characters) are horrified by the implication of 'blow ourselves'
jokes.punchline: His clarification makes things simultaneously clearer (it's definitely not what it sounds like) and more confusing (what is it then, and why is 'across the room' the clarifying detail?). 'There's a video of it!' is an escalation that's also an offer of proof.
jokes.explanation: The best part of this escalation is 'across the room' — it's supposed to prove they were doing separate, independent things, but it maps so specifically onto a sex act that the exculpation fails. 'There's a video' shifts it from anecdote to record, which is an escalation. The full sequence of 62 and 63 is one of the episode's sustained comedy peaks.

jokes.text: 'Are you the secret dumper, brown eyes?' — Marla addressing the dog Brody
jokes.setup: Marla has interrogated all the humans; a new suspect emerges
jokes.punchline: She addresses a dog with the same interrogation style she used on humans — 'the secret dumper, brown eyes' — which is both funny as a detective cliché applied to a dog and as a reveal that the dog was in the apartment all along
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection — 'brown eyes' sounds like a seductive nickname for a human suspect, then Brody the dog emerges. The detective-noir register applied to a dog interrogation is consistent with Marla's character and funny.

jokes.text: Abbi reveals: 'Marla, I know... It was Brody. Come on, Brody! Come on out here, boy.' — throwing the dog under the bus
jokes.setup: Abbi has been trying to avoid being caught as the bathroom culprit
jokes.punchline: She frames the dog as the responsible party, which is both desperate and technically somewhat defensible
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in how quickly and smoothly Abbi throws the dog under the bus — no hesitation, complete commitment to the lie. The reveal that she knows the dog's name (Brody) and can call it by name while framing it for a crime she committed is a perfect character moment.

jokes.text: 'Brody's my dog. And that explains it.' / 'Oh, I feel like a crazy methhead right now.'
jokes.setup: The dog has appeared and Marla accepts this as the explanation
jokes.punchline: Marla's self-reflection — 'I feel like a crazy methhead right now' — is a genuinely funny moment of self-awareness from someone who has been completely unhinged throughout the scene
jokes.explanation: 'Crazy methhead' is a very specific self-descriptor that implies she has a reference class for this behavior. The flash of self-awareness in an otherwise oblivious character is funny because it suggests she's done something like this before and knows what it looks like.

jokes.text: 'Except for one thing! If you had a dog, I'd be dead because I'm allergic, bitch!'
jokes.setup: The crisis appeared to be over — Marla accepted the dog explanation, Abbi seemed safe
jokes.punchline: Marla's dog allergy means the dog story is impossible — she would have reacted. The entire alibi collapses. She adds 'bitch' as an afterthought.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's sharpest joke structurally. The 'Except for one thing!' is a perfect fake-out button on a scene that seemed resolved. The logic is airtight — Marla has a dog allergy, Brody is a dog, Marla has no symptoms, therefore no dog. The alibi is forensically dismantled. 'Bitch' as the capper is doubly funny because (a) it's a profanity aimed at Abbi and (b) it's technically correct as a female dog term, completing the canine theme accidentally. Earns the 8+ because the structure is genuinely sophisticated: a planted alibi that contains its own refutation, revealed at the optimal moment. The phrase 'I'd be dead' is also funny for its drama.

jokes.text: 'So Abbi, why'd you [bleep] in my Shapelys? And tell it to the toe!'
jokes.setup: Marla has deduced it was Abbi and wants a confession
jokes.punchline: 'Tell it to the toe!' — a callback to the mysterious 11th toe introduced at the party's start. Marla uses her extra toe as an authority figure to whom confessions must be made.
jokes.explanation: The best callback in the episode and one of its best jokes overall. 'Tell it to the toe' pays off the mysterious 11th toe setup from the beginning — a detail that seemed like throwaway character weirdness has been transformed into a punchline. The toe is now a judicial authority. The phrase itself is a parody of 'talk to the hand' but upgraded to a misplaced extra toe. This is exemplary callback construction: the setup was planted, the payoff is completely unexpected in its specific form, and it reframes Marla's anatomy as a source of power. It justifies the 8+ score because: the callback was constructed across the entire episode, the payoff is inventive and original, 'tell it to the toe' is instantly quotable, and it could only work on this show with this character.

jokes.text: Abbi's full confession: she pooped in the toilet but couldn't flush (no water), Ilana said she'd handle it, Abbi 'did not think that that meant she would put it in your shoe.'
jokes.setup: The full truth of the doo-doo ninja operation is finally revealed
jokes.punchline: Abbi's moral position: she did the right thing (used the toilet), Ilana did the wrong thing (the shoe). The revelation of her innocence relative to the shoe is also an admission of the bathroom event.
jokes.explanation: The payoff is in the careful distinction Abbi draws between 'what I did' and 'what Ilana did with what I did.' She is both guilty and innocent, and parsing that distinction while mortified is very Abbi. 'I did not think that that meant she would put it in your shoe' is a beautifully legal sentence.

jokes.text: 'Okay, uh, this was so fun. I'm gonna go. Glad I kept my shoes on.'
jokes.setup: Jeremy has just learned the full truth of what happened in the shoe
jokes.punchline: 'Glad I kept my shoes on' — an extremely understated, deadpan response to having been at a party where someone's excrement ended up in someone else's shoe
jokes.explanation: Six words of perfect deadpan. The comedy is in the proportion: Jeremy's only comment on the entire situation is a small gratitude for his own shoe safety. He doesn't address the larger horror, doesn't moralize, just notes the one thing that went right for him personally. 'Glad I kept my shoes on' is extremely quotable and earns its high score because the economy is exceptional and the understatement is calibrated precisely to the maximum absurdity of the situation.

jokes.text: Ilana denies putting anything in a shoe: 'What? / I didn't put it in a shoe.' / Abbi points to shoe with poop. / Ilana: 'There's [bleep] in this shoe, is there not?'
jokes.setup: Abbi confronts Ilana about the shoe disposal method
jokes.punchline: Ilana denies it until physical evidence is presented, then pivots to Socratic acknowledgment: 'There's [bleep] in this shoe, is there not?' — treating it as a philosophical observation rather than a confession
jokes.explanation: 'There's [bleep] in this shoe, is there not?' is a beautifully evasive non-admission. The rhetorical question form distances Ilana from agency — she's merely noting an observable fact, not confessing. 'Is there not?' is particularly funny as a way of confirming something that is definitionally her fault.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Thank you so much for covering for me. You're the best.' / Abbi: 'You [bleep] in your sister's Shapely.' / Ilana: 'Just trying to say thank you.'
jokes.setup: Abbi confronts Ilana about the shoe
jokes.punchline: Ilana is thanking Bevers (or whoever) for covering for her, as though the shoe disposal was an act of friendship and what followed was a normal cover-up situation
jokes.explanation: 'Just trying to say thank you' as a response to 'you [bleep] in your sister's Shapely' is a perfect Ilana deflection — she acknowledges the confrontation but pivots immediately to the good she was trying to do. Her moral universe remains intact.

jokes.text: Lincoln tells Ilana he has a 'man crush' on Jeremy; Ilana agrees: 'Jeremy has amazing teeth.'
jokes.setup: Lincoln, Ilana's sort-of-boyfriend, has just watched the whole chaos of the evening
jokes.punchline: He bonds with Ilana over Jeremy's attractiveness — they are both openly appreciating another man together, which is both sweet and funny as a relationship beat
jokes.explanation: 'Jeremy has amazing teeth' is a specific, unexpected feature to identify — not eyes, not body, teeth. The dental appreciation is a funny choice. The male bonding over another man's attractiveness is consistent with Ilana's non-possessive approach to relationships.

jokes.text: Jaime's hurricane confession: as a child, his pet turtle's private name was the N-word. 'I would say, Come here, [bleep]. I love you, [bleep].'
jokes.setup: 'Before we die, there is something I have to say to you and it's very hard for me to say.' — maximum gravitas buildup
jokes.punchline: Jaime's darkest secret is that he named his turtle a racial slur — from ignorance, as a child, apparently with genuine affection. The 'I love you' phrasing makes it worse and funnier simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: Dark and structurally well-built. The hurricane confession frame has been established; Jaime's feels the most significant ('very hard for me to say'). The turtle reveal is funny because of the combination of deep shame and apparent childhood innocence — he loved the turtle, he just named it horrifically. The impact is in the reaction beats (Lincoln going quiet). The show handles this with a clear framing that it's about Jaime's ignorance and Lincoln's discomfort, not endorsement. It's genuinely uncomfortable and structured to be.

jokes.text: CALLBACK REPLAY: 'Get out there and bounce them little titties for that hunky, quarter-Latino' — shown again as recap/replay
jokes.setup: The episode appears to replay key comedic moments
jokes.punchline: The line lands again in context with what we now know happened afterward
jokes.explanation: Replay sequences are inherently lower craft than original jokes — the work was done in the original setup. The callback's function is structural (reminding the audience of the setup) but its comedy value is derivative. Scored lower accordingly.

jokes.text: CALLBACK REPLAY: 'It is gone. The deed is done. / It looks like there's a talent show / In the living room' — shown again
jokes.setup: Second replay sequence of earlier comedy beats
jokes.punchline: Hearing 'it is gone, the deed is done' with knowledge of the shoe makes the song that follows funnier retroactively
jokes.explanation: Same reasoning as #75 — replay content is structurally lower craft. The sequence works better in context but doesn't generate new comedy.
